ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a class for french fries (FOODON_00002694) but no specific class for loaded/topped fries with bolognaise sauce and cheese. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fried potato products; no specific code exists for bolognaise-topped fries. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for bolognaise fries as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard café serving of bolognaise fries is approximately 300 g, comprising a base of deep-fried potato fries topped with beef bolognaise sauce, melted mozzarella, and nacho cheese sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central component foods: French fries (fast food, SR Legacy 21138), Beef bolognese sauce (SR Legacy 27036), Mozzarella cheese (SR Legacy 01026), Nacho cheese sauce (SR Legacy 43408); weighted composite at approximate proportions 55% fries, 30% bolognaise sauce, 10% mozzarella, 5% nacho cheese sauce.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for bolognaise fries exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or other approved primary sources. Values are estimated by weighted composite of four SR Legacy component foods: french fries (55%), beef bolognese sauce (30%), mozzarella (10%), nacho cheese sauce (5%). Per-100g values derived from this blend. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of french fries and analogous potato-based composite dishes; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283. French fries GI = 63 (glucose reference). Composite dish GI estimated at 63 based on dominant carbohydrate source (potato fries) with minor downward adjustment from protein and fat co-ingestion.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bolognaise fries as a composite dish. GI of 63 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for french fries (fast food), which is the dominant carbohydrate-contributing component. The addition of beef bolognaise sauce, mozzarella, and nacho cheese sauce adds protein and fat, which may modestly lower the effective GI; however, the published french fries GI of 63 is retained as a conservative estimate. GL per serving calculated as (63 × 64.5 g available carbohydrate per 300 g serving) / 100 = 40.7, rounded to 40.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~390 mg/100 g; ~1170 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single 300 g serving provides approximately 1170 mg sodium, representing roughly 50% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or avoid this dish on high-sodium days.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (~40.8 per serving) driven by deep-fried potato base
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium GI (63) combined with a large carbohydrate load (~64.5 g per 300 g serving) produces a high glycemic load of approximately 40.8, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or substantially reduce portion size and pair with low-GI foods.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.9 g/100 g), trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g), high sodium, and deep-frying method
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat from cheese and beef sauce, residual trans fat from deep-frying, and high sodium content makes this dish unfavourable for cardiovascular health; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit consumption and choose lower-fat, lower-sodium alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bolognaise Fries is a Western-style café snack dish popular in Singapore, consisting of a generous base of deep-fried potato fries topped with a savoury beef bolognaise sauce, melted mozzarella cheese, and a drizzle of nacho cheese sauce. The dish combines the crispy, starchy texture of french fries with the rich, meaty umami of slow-cooked beef bolognaise, the mild creaminess of mozzarella, and the sharp, processed saltiness of nacho cheese. It is typically served as a sharing snack or indulgent side dish in café settings. Nutritionally, a standard 300 g serving delivers approximately 594 kcal, 21.6 g protein, 29.4 g fat, and 64.5 g carbohydrate, with a high sodium load of approximately 1170 mg. The glycemic load per serving is high (~40.8), driven by the deep-fried potato base. The dish is not suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without significant portion restriction. It contains dairy (mozzarella, nacho cheese) and is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten. In Singapore, this dish is commonly found in Western-themed cafés and casual dining outlets on food delivery platforms such as GrabFood.
common_user_queries: How many calories in bolognaise fries?
What is the sodium content of bolognaise fries?
What cheese is on bolognaise fries?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for bolognaise fries exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of four USDA SR Legacy component foods at approximate proportions. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component (french fries) using the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence reflects the estimation methodology and absence of a direct analytical match.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this composite estimate. All other micronutrient values are estimated from the weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for french fries (21138), beef bolognese sauce (27036), mozzarella (01026), and nacho cheese sauce (43408) at proportions 55:30:10:5. Serving size of 300 g is an estimate based on typical café portion sizes for loaded fries in Singapore; actual restaurant portion may vary. Halal status is listed as false because it cannot be confirmed from available data; the beef source and cheese rennet type are unknown. The cuisine tag in the input JSON was 'Coffee' (likely a GrabFood platform category for café-style food); the canonical cuisine has been corrected to 'Western' as this is a Western-style dish. GI source match type is analogous_estimate; no direct GI measurement for bolognaise fries as a composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature.
